text: The surgeon begins the operation with trocars in place, identifying the ligament of Treitz at the base of the transverse mesocolon. The small bowel is transected 40 centimeters from this point, and a side-to-side jejunojejunostomy is created. The anastomosis is visible, followed by closure of the enterotomy and mesenteric defect to prevent internal hernia formation. The roux limb, approximately 20 centimeters in length, is prepared to be brought in a retrocolic fashion after exposing the pancreas and opening the pancreatic duct.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac at the greater curve of the stomach, detaching the greater omentum to access the pancreas. The posterior wall of the stomach is visible, and the pancreas is identified, appearing firm with inflammation. A needle withdraws clear fluid from the pancreatic duct, followed by the use of hook cautery on the anterior wall of the pancreas to access the duct. A Maryland dissector is used to access the duct, and the hook cautery opens the duct, revealing its wide channel down towards the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses hook cautery to incise the firm anterior wall of the pancreas, demonstrating the gland's dense texture. The roux limb is positioned in a retrocolic fashion with its blind end towards the tail of the pancreas. An enterotomy is made in the roux limb, extending along the length of the pancreatic ductotomy.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis of the pancreatic duct to the small bowel using a running PDS suture. The barbed suture is pulled through the tissue, locking it in place. The suture is anchored at the tail of the pancreas and runs along the inferior border of the ductotomy. The firm texture of the pancreas is visible as the needle is driven through, and the suture is pulled taut to secure the small bowel against the pancreas. Due to a size mismatch, the ductotomy is extended towards the head of the pancreas to align with the aneurotomy length.

The surgeon continues with full thickness sutures through both the small bowel and the pancreatic wall, reaching the apex of the inferior wall of the pancreas. The suture is brought back through the small bowel, utilizing the barbed nature to lock it in place. Needle trauma is visible due to calcification within the pancreatic wall, highlighting the firm texture of the pancreas which aids in holding the sutures securely.

The surgeon runs a second suture from the tail of the pancreas, attaching the small bowel to the pancreatic wall for the superior aspect of the anastomosis. The suture lines overlap at the apex, and the barbed suture locks in place without needing to be tied. The roux limb is then tacked at the transverse mesocolon to prevent internal hernias.
